analogy: Think of "Population Composition" through the chapter's own opening images: Unit-II Chapter-3 Human Development Human Development Human Development Human Development Human Development The words ‘growth’ and ‘development’ are not new to you. Look around you, almost everything that you can see (and many that you cannot) grows and develops. These may be plants, cities, ideas,... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Population Composition is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that unit-II Chapter-3 Human Development Human Development Human Development Human Development Human Development The words ‘growth’ and ‘development’ are not new to you.

The text explains that look around you, almost everything that you can see (and many that you cannot) grows and develops.

The text explains that these may be plants, cities, ideas, nations, relationships or even you yourself!

The text explains that do growth and development mean the same thing?

The text explains that this chapter discusses the concept of human development as it pertains to nations and communities.

The text explains that gROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT Both growth and development refer to changes over a period of time.

The text explains that the difference is that growth is quantitative and value neutral.

The text explains that this means that the change may be either positive (showing an increase) or negative (indicating a decrease).

The text explains that development means a qualitative change which is always value positive.

The text explains that this means that development cannot take place unless there is an increment or addition to the existing conditions.

The text explains that development occurs when positive growth takes place.

The text explains that yet, positive growth does not always lead to development.

The text explains that development occurs when there is a positive change in quality.

The text explains that for example, if the population of a city grows from one lakh to two lakhs over a period of time, we say the city has grown.

The text explains that however, if a facilities like housing, provision of basic services and other characteristics remain the same, then this growth has not been accompanied by development.

The text explains that can you think of a few more examples to differentiate between growth and development?

The text explains that therefore, access to resources, health and education are the key areas in human development.

The text explains that building people’s capabilities in the areas of health, education and access to resources is therefore, important in enlarging their choices.

The text explains that similarly, very often poor people cannot choose to take medical treatment for disease because their choice is limited by their lack of resources.

The text explains that all environmental, financial and human resources must be used keeping in mind the future.

The text explains that misuse of any of these resources will lead to fewer opportunities for future generations.

The text explains that mEASURING HUMAN DEVELOPMENT The human development index (HDI) ranks the countries based on their performance in the key areas of health, education and access to resources.

The text explains that these rankings are based on a score between 0 to 1 that a country earns from its record in the key areas of human development.

The text explains that access to resources is measured in terms of purchasing power (in U.S. dollars).

The text explains that the closer a score is to one, the greater is the level of human development.

The text explains that therefore, a score of 0.983 would be considered very high while 0.268 would mean a very low level of human development.

The text explains that building human capabilities in the areas of health, education and access to resources is the key to increasing human development.

The text explains that size of the territory and per capita income are not directly related to human development.

The text explains that countries can be classified into four groups on the basis of the human development scores earned by them (Table 3.2).

The text explains that many of these countries have been rapidly improving their human development score by adopting more people-oriented policies and reducing social discrimination.

The text explains that most of these countries have a much higher social diversity than the countries with higher human development scores.

The text explains that many in this group have Countries with very high human development index are those which have a score of over 0.800.

The text explains that sweden Source: Human Development Report, 2023-24 Try to locate these countries on a map.

The text explains that many of the countries with a high human development score are located in Europe and represent the industrialised western world.

The text explains that distribution of the country’s resources is also far more equitable.

The text explains that compare their scores on the human development index.

The text explains that for example, X country has lower human development because its people follow Y religion, or belong to Z community.

The text explains that to understand why a particular region keeps reporting low or high levels of human development it is important to look at the pattern of government expenditure on the social sector.

The text explains that the political environment of the country and the amount of freedom people have is also important.

The text explains that countries with high levels of human development invest more in the social sectors and are generally free from political turmoil and instability.

The text explains that on the other hand, places with low levels of human development tend to spend more on defence rather than social sectors.

The text explains that this shows that these countries tend to be located in areas of political instability and have not been able to initiate accelerated economic development.

The text explains that human Development 21 Project/Activity Project/Activity Project/Activity Project/Activity Project/Activity Make a list of the ten most corrupt countries and ten least corrupt countries.

The text explains that consult the latest Human Development Report for this.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography, Chapter 3: Population Composition

This chapter discusses the concept of human development as it pertains to nations and communities. (NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography Ch3 pg 1)
GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT Both growth and development refer to changes over a period of time. (NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography Ch3 pg 1)
The difference is that growth is quantitative and value neutral. (NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography Ch3 pg 1)
This means that the change may be either positive (showing an increase) or negative (indicating a decrease). (NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography Ch3 pg 1)
Development means a qualitative change which is always value positive. (NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography Ch3 pg 1)
This means that development cannot take place unless there is an increment or addition to the existing conditions. (NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography Ch3 pg 1)

cases.name: Unit
cases.point: Unit-II Chapter-3 Human Development Human Development Human Development Human Development Human Development The words ‘growth’ and ‘development’ are not new to you. (NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography Ch3 pg 1)

cases.name: Fundamentals
cases.point: Fundamentals of Human Geography 14 economic growth. (NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography Ch3 pg 1)

cases.name: The
cases.point: The works of two South Asian economists, Mahbub-ul-Haq and Amartya Sen are important in this regard. (NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography Ch3 pg 1)

cases.name: The
cases.point: The concept of human development was introduced by Dr Mahbub-ul-Haq. (NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography Ch3 pg 1)

cases.name: Dr Haq
cases.point: Dr Haq has described human development as development that enlarges people’s choices and improves their lives. (NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography Ch3 pg 1)

schemes.name: The United Nations Development Programme
schemes.point: The United Nations Development Programme has used his concept of human development to publish the Human Development Report annually since 1990. (NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography Ch3 pg 1)

schemes.name: The Government
schemes.point: The Government of India has introduced Beti Bachao Beti Padhao programme to address the issue of decline in child sex ratio. (NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography Ch3 pg 2)

schemes.name: The
schemes.point: The approach argues for higher government expenditure on education, health, social secondary and amenities. (NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography Ch3 pg 4)

schemes.name: The
schemes.point: The government is responsible for increasing levels of human development by maximising expenditure on welfare. (NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography Ch3 pg 4)

schemes.name: Basic Needs Approach This
schemes.point: (c) Basic Needs Approach This approach was initially proposed by the International Labour Organisation (ILO). (NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography Ch3 pg 4)

prelims_traps: Do not treat GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT and THE FOUR PILLARS OF HUMAN as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Population Composition; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Population Composition through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT and THE FOUR PILLARS OF HUMAN to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
